sections.header: Verses 1-6 — "The Vision of the Great Prostitute"

heb.word: τὸ κρίμα τῆς πόρνης τῆς μεγάλης
heb.transliteration: to krima tēs pornēs tēs megalēs
heb.gloss: the judgment of the great prostitute
heb.paragraph: The noun pornē (prostitute, harlot) carries a long Old Testament pedigree as a metaphor for idolatrous and apostate cities. Hosea applied it to Israel (Hos 1–3); Isaiah to Jerusalem (Isa 1:21); Nahum to Nineveh (Nah 3:4); Ezekiel to both Samaria and Jerusalem (Ezek 16, 23). The adjective megalē (great) elevates this figure beyond any single historical city to a symbolic representation of the entire world system in its seductive, idolatrous opposition to God. The genitive construction to krima tēs pornēs (the judgment of the prostitute) is an objective genitive—judgment pronounced upon her, not by her.

The term mystērion (mystery, secret) in apocalyptic literature denotes not something unknowable but something previously hidden that is now divinely revealed. Paul uses it for the inclusion of Gentiles (Eph 3:3–6); John uses it for the identity of Babylon. The forehead inscription 'MYSTERY' (v. 5) may be titular (labeling the name as symbolic) or substantive (mystery itself is part of the name). Either way, it signals that Babylon's identity requires divinely aided interpretation—surface appearances deceive.

refs.note: Jeremiah addressed historical Babylon as dwelling 'by abundant waters, rich in treasures.' Revelation 17:1 transfers this description to the eschatological Babylon who 'sits on many waters,' later identified as 'peoples, multitudes, nations and languages' (17:15). The shift from geographical description to symbolic interpretation reveals that Babylon has become a transhistorical symbol for the world system in its opposition to God.

Ezekiel's extended prostitution metaphor for Jerusalem describes a city that took the gifts God gave her—beauty, clothing, jewelry—and used them for idolatrous purposes. The great prostitute of Revelation 17 is similarly adorned with royal luxury (purple, scarlet, gold, precious stones) that she has used to seduce the nations into spiritual adultery. The parallel suggests that Babylon represents not merely pagan Rome but the universal human tendency to corrupt divine gifts into instruments of idolatry.

Daniel's fourth beast—terrifying, with iron teeth and ten horns—provides the template for the scarlet beast that carries the prostitute. The ten horns of Daniel 7 represent successive kings; in Revelation 17:12, the ten horns are ten kings who share authority with the beast for 'one hour.' The intertextual link identifies Babylon's political power as the culmination of Daniel's four-kingdom scheme.

notes.note: The great prostitute represents the final form of false religion that will dominate the tribulation period. She 'sits on many waters'—symbolizing her global influence over peoples and nations (v. 15). The kings of the earth have committed adultery with her, indicating the collusion between political power and religious apostasy. This is not merely spiritual metaphor; it describes an actual, identifiable religious system that will emerge in the end times.

The scarlet beast she rides is the Antichrist himself, covered with blasphemous names. Her purple and scarlet clothing, gold jewelry, and jeweled cup point to immense wealth and external beauty masking internal corruption. The cup is 'filled with abominable things and the filth of her adulteries'—the intoxicating appeal of false religion conceals spiritual death within.

The name on her forehead—'BABYLON THE GREAT, THE MOTHER OF PROSTITUTES AND OF THE ABOMINATIONS OF THE EARTH'—identifies her as the source of all organized false religion throughout history. From the Tower of Babel to the final apostate system, Babylon has been the fountainhead of idolatry, occultism, and religious corruption.

She is 'drunk with the blood of God's holy people, the blood of those who bore testimony to Jesus.' This is not figurative—she is responsible for the systematic persecution and martyrdom of believers throughout the tribulation. John's astonishment suggests that the prostitute's identity will be shocking, perhaps because she masquerades as true religion.

The prostitute seated upon the beast reveals the essential nature of all false religion: it depends upon secular power for its authority and, in turn, legitimizes that power's excesses. Calvin saw this dynamic vividly in the medieval papacy's alliance with temporal rulers. The broader principle is that wherever religious institutions derive their authority from political accommodation rather than from God's Word, they become prostitutes regardless of their external splendor. The purple and scarlet, the gold and jewels—these are the trappings of a church that has exchanged spiritual authority for worldly grandeur.

That the prostitute is 'the mother of prostitutes' indicates that false religion is self-propagating: it spawns imitations and variations of itself across cultures and centuries. Calvin would note that Babel's original sin—building a tower to make a name apart from God (Gen 11:4)—is the prototype of all subsequent religious systems that seek human glory rather than divine worship. Her intoxication with the blood of saints reveals the ultimate trajectory of false religion: it inevitably persecutes true faith because true faith exposes its fraudulence.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase tēs pornēs tēs megalēs (the great prostitute) uses the articular construction indicating a well-known figure. The participle kathēmenēs epi hydatōn pollōn (sitting upon many waters) alludes to Jeremiah 51:13 (LXX 28:13), the only OT passage applying this exact phrase to Babylon. The shift from literal geography (Euphrates canal system) to symbolic meaning (peoples and nations, 17:15) is characteristic of John's typological hermeneutic.

The inscription epi to metōpon autēs onoma gegrammenon (on her forehead a name written) may reflect Roman prostitution practices, where prostitutes wore headbands bearing their names. The word mystērion is textually uncertain—some manuscripts read it as part of the name, others as a separate label indicating that the name is symbolic. The UBS committee rated the inclusion with a {C} confidence level.

beale.source: G.K. Beale, Revelation, NIGTC (1999) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Beale reads the prostitute as a comprehensive symbol for the socio-economic-religious system of the world in its seductive opposition to God—not reducible to Rome alone, though Rome is the primary first-century referent. The woman's attire (purple, scarlet, gold, precious stones) echoes the high priest's vestments (Exod 28:5–6, 15–20), suggesting a parody of true worship. She is an anti-church, a counter-bride to the Lamb's wife (19:7–8; 21:2, 9–11). The 'many waters' derive from Jeremiah's Babylon (Jer 51:13), but John interprets them as peoples and nations (17:15), universalizing the symbol. Her intoxication with martyrs' blood identifies persecution as the fundamental activity of the Babylonian system: wherever the true church is opposed, Babylon is present.

osborne.source: Grant R. Osborne, Revelation, BECNT (2002) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Osborne situates the vision within the broader literary structure of Revelation, noting the chiastic parallel between the prostitute vision (17:1–19:10) and the bride vision (21:9–22:9)—both introduced by one of the seven bowl angels saying 'Come, I will show you.' This structural pairing reveals that the two women represent two mutually exclusive allegiances: Babylon or New Jerusalem, the prostitute or the bride. The scarlet beast she rides is the same beast from chapter 13, now explicitly linked to the abyss (v. 8) and destined for destruction. Osborne notes that John's great astonishment (v. 6b) is unusual—prophetic seers are typically stoic observers—suggesting that something about Babylon's identity is deeply surprising, perhaps her resemblance to the true church.

hist.context: The vision of the great prostitute seated on the scarlet beast initiates a detailed exposition of Babylon that extends through chapter 18. One of the seven bowl angels serves as guide (v. 1), connecting this vision directly to the bowl judgments of chapter 16. The prostitute imagery draws on the prophetic tradition of depicting unfaithful cities as harlots—Isaiah's Jerusalem (Isa 1:21), Ezekiel's Samaria and Jerusalem (Ezek 16, 23), and Nahum's Nineveh (Nah 3:4). The woman sits on 'many waters' (v. 1), echoing Jeremiah's description of Babylon as dwelling 'by abundant waters' (Jer 51:13). Her scarlet and purple attire, gold jewelry, and golden cup filled with abominations evoke both royal luxury and cultic impurity. The detail that she is 'drunk with the blood of God's holy people' (v. 6) identifies her as the persecuting power behind the martyrdom of the saints. John's astonishment (ethaumasa... thauma mega, 'I marveled... greatly') signals that Babylon's identity is not immediately obvious—it requires the angelic interpretation that follows.

heb.word: ἦν καὶ οὐκ ἔστιν καὶ μέλλει ἀναβαίνειν ἐκ τῆς ἀβύσσου
heb.transliteration: ēn kai ouk estin kai mellei anabainein ek tēs abyssou
heb.gloss: was, and is not, and is about to come up out of the abyss
heb.paragraph: This triple temporal formula parodies the divine name 'who is, who was, and who is to come' (1:4, 8; 4:8). The beast 'was' (past existence), 'is not' (present apparent death), and 'is about to ascend from the abyss' (future resurrection). This is a satanic counterfeit of Christ's death and resurrection. The imperfect ēn (was) and present ouk estin (is not) are straightforward, but mellei anabainein (is about to ascend) uses the verb mellō with the present infinitive, indicating imminence—the beast's return is near, and when it comes, it will astonish those whose names are not written in the book of life.

refs.note: Daniel's prophecy that 'the God of heaven will set up a kingdom that will never be destroyed' provides the eschatological framework for the ten kings' brief reign. Their authority lasts 'one hour' (Rev 17:12) because their kingdoms are temporary clay mixed with iron—they cannot endure against the stone cut without human hands (Dan 2:34–35, 44). The Lamb conquers them because He is 'Lord of lords and King of kings' (Rev 17:14).

Ezekiel prophesied that Jerusalem's former lovers would turn against her: 'I will bring them against you from every side.' The same dynamic operates in Revelation 17:16—the beast and the ten horns turn against the prostitute and destroy her. The theological principle is consistent: God uses the instruments of sin as instruments of judgment against sin itself.

notes.note: The beast 'who once was, now is not, and yet will come' is the Antichrist who will experience a death-and-resurrection that mimics Christ's own. This will be either a genuine satanic resurrection or a convincing simulation designed to deceive the world. The inhabitants of the earth will be astonished—this miracle will be the catalyst for worldwide worship of the beast.

The seven heads are both seven hills (Rome) and seven kings, representing successive world empires. The 'five have fallen' likely refers to Egypt, Assyria, Babylon, Medo-Persia, and Greece; the 'one is' to Rome; and the 'other has not yet come' to the revived Roman Empire of the end times. The beast itself is an 'eighth' who belongs to the seven—the final Antichrist who embodies the total power of all previous world empires.

The ten horns are ten future kings who will form a confederacy under the Antichrist's leadership during the tribulation. Their authority 'for one hour' emphasizes the brevity of their reign. Despite their combined military power, they will wage war against the Lamb and be defeated—because the Lamb is 'Lord of lords and King of kings.' This is the clearest title of divine sovereignty applied to Christ in Revelation.

The prostitute's destruction by the very beast she rode is God's sovereign irony. The political powers she used for her own purposes will turn against her and consume her. This is God putting it 'into their hearts to accomplish his purpose' (v. 17)—even the actions of the wicked serve God's decree. The identification of the woman as 'the great city that rules over the kings of the earth' (v. 18) points unmistakably to a specific, identifiable entity in the end times.

The angel's interpretation reveals that evil empires follow a pattern of rise, apparent death, and resurgence. Calvin would recognize here the Reformed principle that human sin produces the same political pathologies across every age—tyranny, idolatry, and persecution are not unique to any single empire but recur wherever humanity organizes power apart from God. The parody of the divine name ('was, is not, will come') exposes the beast as a counterfeit god who can only imitate, never originate. His power is derivative and therefore doomed.

That God puts it into the hearts of the ten kings to destroy the prostitute is a stunning affirmation of divine sovereignty over evil. Calvin emphasized that God does not merely permit evil but governs it—directing the actions of the wicked to serve His purposes without being the author of their sin. The prostitute's destruction by her own allies demonstrates the self-destructive nature of all alliances built on mutual exploitation rather than divine covenant.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The tripartite formula ēn kai ouk estin kai parestai (was and is not and will be present) presents a textual variant: some manuscripts read mellei anabainein (is about to ascend) instead of parestai (will be present). The variant anabainein ek tēs abyssou (ascend from the abyss) explicitly connects the beast's return with the demonic realm, while parestai merely indicates future presence. The critical editions favor the longer reading with the abyss reference.

The title Kyrios kyriōn kai Basileus basileōn (Lord of lords and King of kings) reverses the word order of 19:16 (King of kings and Lord of lords). Both formulations echo Deuteronomy 10:17 and Daniel 2:47, applying the supreme sovereignty title to the Lamb. The future tense nikēsei (will conquer) anticipates the battle scene of 19:11–21.

beale.source: G.K. Beale, Revelation, NIGTC (1999) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Beale argues that the seven heads/kings should not be identified with specific historical rulers but understood as a symbolic sevenfold totality of world empires in their opposition to God. The number seven represents completeness; the beast embodies the full weight of all anti-God political power throughout history. The 'eighth' who belongs to the seven is the beast in his final, eschatological manifestation—the ultimate concentration of totalitarian power. The parody of God's eternal name reveals the beast as a cosmic impersonator whose imitation of Christ's death and resurrection (13:3) is the supreme deception of the dragon's anti-kingdom strategy.

The self-destructive collapse of the Babylonian system is a recurrent Old Testament motif: God turns the enemies of His people against each other (Judg 7:22; 2 Chr 20:22–23; Ezek 38:21). Beale sees this as the fulfillment of the prophetic principle that evil contains the seeds of its own destruction. God does not need to intervene externally; He orchestrates the internal contradictions of the anti-God system so that it consumes itself. This is the ultimate expression of divine sovereignty: even the beast's own rage serves God's purpose (v. 17).

osborne.source: Grant R. Osborne, Revelation, BECNT (2002) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Osborne surveys the major interpretive options for the seven kings: preterist (specific Roman emperors), historicist (successive empires), and symbolic (totality of anti-God power). He favors a modified historicist-symbolic reading: the seven represent the complete succession of world powers opposing God's people, with the 'eighth' being the final Antichrist who embodies them all. The 'was, is not, will come' formula is best understood as a deliberate parody of the divine name, revealing the beast as a counterfeit deity whose power, unlike God's, is discontinuous and dependent on the abyss for renewal.

Osborne highlights the theological irony: the prostitute who used the beast for her own purposes is destroyed by the beast she rode. This self-destructive dynamic is not accidental but divinely orchestrated (v. 17). The principle extends beyond the specific narrative to all alliances between religious and political power that are built on mutual exploitation. When God's purposes are served, He dissolves the alliance by turning one partner against the other.

hist.context: The angel's interpretation (vv. 7–18) deciphers the vision's symbolism in a passage that has generated more interpretive debate than almost any other in Revelation. The beast 'was, and now is not, and yet will come' (v. 8) is widely recognized as a parody of God's own self-designation (1:4, 8). The seven heads are identified as seven hills (v. 9)—a nearly unmistakable reference to Rome, the city on seven hills—and also as seven kings (v. 10). The ten horns are ten kings who receive authority with the beast 'for one hour' (v. 12), a compressed timeframe emphasizing the brevity and futility of their power. The most striking element is the self-destructive reversal of verses 16–17: the beast and the ten horns will turn against the prostitute and destroy her. This internal collapse of the evil system is attributed to God's sovereign purpose—'God has put it into their hearts to accomplish his purpose' (v. 17). The chapter thus reveals that Babylon's destruction is not merely the result of divine intervention from without but of divinely orchestrated self-destruction from within.

rows.label: v.1
rows.text: Angel introduces the great prostitute vision

rows.label: vv.1-2
rows.text: Setting: prostitute on many waters, kings committed adultery

rows.label: vv.3-4
rows.text: Vision: woman on scarlet beast, adorned in luxury

rows.label: vv.5-6
rows.text: Name revealed; drunk with blood of saints

rows.label: vv.7-8
rows.text: Angel begins interpretation: the beast that was, is not, will come

rows.label: vv.9-11
rows.text: Seven heads = seven hills/kings; the eighth king

rows.label: vv.12-14
rows.text: Ten horns = ten kings; war against the Lamb

rows.label: vv.15-18
rows.text: Many waters = peoples; beast destroys the prostitute

lit.note: The chapter follows the vision-interpretation pattern common in apocalyptic literature (cf. Daniel 7–8): the seer receives a symbolic vision (vv. 1–6), then an angelic interpreter decodes it (vv. 7–18). The interpretation itself moves from the beast (vv. 7–11) to the horns (vv. 12–14) to the waters and the woman (vv. 15–18), proceeding from the supporting structure to the figure it supports.

themes.note: Revelation 17 exposes the nature of false religion and its alliance with political power. Divine sovereignty dominates as God reveals that even the beast's self-destructive rampage serves His purpose (v. 17). The contrast between the prostitute and the Lamb's bride (anticipated in 19:7–8) sets up the fundamental choice of allegiance that runs throughout Revelation.

coaching.tip: Babylon the prostitute, drunk on saints' blood, riding the beast — the empire that seduces and destroys. She's adorned with wealth but filled with abominations (v. 4). The imagery exposes what the world admires. Prosperity can fund persecution. Beauty can mask brutality.

'The ten horns... will hate the prostitute and make her desolate' (v. 16). The beast turns on Babylon. Evil alliances self-destruct. Sin contains the seeds of its own destruction. What seemed stable collapses from within. God uses wickedness against wickedness. Babylon falls to her own partners.
